evidence_excerpt: After initial hematologic remission (BM D+23), PCR later became detectable prompting TKI change to nilotinib and escalation to planned blinatumomab; she also had recurrent pleural effusions requiring multiple thoracenteses and inpatient treatment complicated by neurotoxicity (mental status changes) requiring dose adjustment.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Ongoing fatigue, mild rib pain attributed to pleural effusions, dry cough, and mild SOB; pleural effusions requiring multiple thoracenteses.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: Bone marrow biopsy on D+23 consistent with hematologic remission; discharged home after achieving remission.
PCR became detectable, leading to switch from dasatinib to nilotinib.
Developed recurrent pleural effusions requiring management.
Multiple thoracenteses performed for pleural effusions.
During monitored inpatient treatment, had slight headache, low-grade fever, and mental status changes requiring a change in medication dose.
Neurotoxicity symptoms improved after dose adjustment; most recent PCR undetectable.

notes_on_uncertainty: Exact dates are not provided; events are placed relative to the diagnosis. It is unclear whether blinatumomab was fully initiated during this admission and whether nilotinib was briefly held or continued, and no post-discharge long-term follow-up is available. Mapping molecular response (PCR changes) to symptom_improvement is a proxy for disease status change rather than direct symptom change.
